questions.prompt: Which one is written correctly as a complete sentence?
questions.hint: Check BOTH things: does it start with a capital letter AND end with a period?
questions.whyHard: Three distractors each break just one rule (missing capital, missing end mark, or both), so the child must check the beginning and the end together.

questions.prompt: One of these sentences is asking something. Which one should end with a question mark?
questions.hint: Read each one and ask: is it telling me something, or is it asking me something?
questions.whyHard: None of the choices show an end mark, so the child can't spot the answer by punctuation — they must recognize a question by its meaning.

questions.prompt: Which group of words is a COMPLETE sentence?
questions.hint: A complete sentence needs a naming part (who) AND an action or telling part (what they do).
questions.whyHard: Every distractor is a tempting phrase that's missing either the who or the action, so the child must feel what makes a thought complete, not just count words.

questions.prompt: How many sentences are here?  "We ran fast. It was fun. Let's go again!"
questions.hint: Count the end marks — each period, question mark, or exclamation point closes one sentence.
questions.whyHard: The child must use capital letters and end marks as sentence boundaries and count them, instead of just reading straight through.

questions.prompt: This sentence is missing a capital letter: "my friend has a dog."  Which word should start with a capital letter?
questions.hint: A capital letter goes on the very FIRST word of the sentence — not on the most important word.
questions.whyHard: Kids often capitalize the 'important' noun like friend or dog; the rule is that the first word gets the capital no matter how small it is.

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /s/ ... /t/ ... /r/ ... /a/ ... /p/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Say each sound slowly and don't skip any. Listen for THREE sounds crashing together at the start before the vowel.
questions.whyHard: A three-consonant beginning blend (str) where kids often drop the middle /t/ or /r/, and 'trap'/'scrap' are close real-word traps.

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /d/ ... /r/ ... /i/ ... /f/ ... /t/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: There is a blend at the START and a different blend at the END. Say the last two sounds carefully after the vowel.
questions.whyHard: Two consonant blends in one word (dr- and -ft); distractors each keep only one blend so the child must hold both.

questions.prompt: Which word BEGINS with a consonant blend (two sounds you can hear separately)? shed, chin, sled, thin
questions.hint: A blend lets you hear each letter's sound. A digraph like sh, ch, or th is two letters that make just ONE sound.
questions.whyHard: Forces the child to separate true blends (two sounds) from digraphs (one sound) that also start with two letters.

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /s/ ... /p/ ... /l/ ... /a/ ... /sh/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Start with three sounds blended together, then the vowel, then one ending sound made by two letters (sh).
questions.whyHard: A three-letter opening blend (spl) plus an ending digraph (sh); 'slash'/'splat' drop or swap exactly one sound.

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /b/ ... /l/ ... /e/ ... /n/ ... /d/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Count the sounds: there is a blend at the beginning AND a blend at the end. Every sound must show up in your word.
questions.whyHard: Opening blend bl- and closing blend -nd together; each wrong choice quietly drops one of the four consonant sounds.

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word **flag**?
questions.hint: Say the word very slowly. A beginning blend like 'fl' is really TWO sounds squished together — pull off just the very first one.
questions.whyHard: The 'fl' blend tempts kids to name two sounds as one; only the single first phoneme /f/ is correct.

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word **shell**?
questions.hint: Watch out — two letters can team up to make ONE brand-new sound. Say the start of the word and listen for a single hiss, not /s/ then /h/.
questions.whyHard: 'sh' is a digraph (one sound), so the tempting /s/ and /h/ answers are wrong even though those letters are right there.

questions.prompt: What is the MIDDLE sound in the word **chin**?
questions.hint: The middle sound is always the vowel. Skip past the beginning sound (even if it's a two-letter team) and find the vowel in the center.
questions.whyHard: Kids must treat 'ch' as one beginning sound, then isolate the medial vowel /i/ rather than picking a consonant.

questions.prompt: What is the LAST sound in the word **hand**?
questions.hint: Stretch the word to the very end. An ending blend like 'nd' has two sounds — you want only the last little one you hear.
questions.whyHard: The final 'nd' blend makes /nd/ and /n/ feel right, but the single ending phoneme is /d/.

questions.prompt: What is the LAST sound in the word **fish**?
questions.hint: Two letters at the end can make ONE sound. Say the word and hold the very last sound — is it a hiss-shush, or two separate sounds?
questions.whyHard: 'sh' is a digraph, so the ending is one /sh/ phoneme, not the /s/ or /h/ the letters suggest.

questions.prompt: Say the word **ship** slowly. How many separate sounds do you hear?
questions.hint: Stretch it out: /sh/.../i/.../p/. Remember that two letters can team up to make just one sound.
questions.whyHard: The 'sh' digraph is 2 letters but only 1 sound, so the letter count fools you.

questions.prompt: Say the word **fox** slowly. How many separate sounds do you hear?
questions.hint: Stretch the ending: /f/.../o/.../k/.../s/. Listen for what the letter x really says.
questions.whyHard: The single letter x actually spells two sounds, /k/ + /s/, so 3-letter 'fox' has 4 sounds.

questions.prompt: Which one of these words has exactly **five** sounds?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and tap once for every sound. Watch out for letter teams like 'sh', 'th', 'ch', and 'ck' that make just one sound.
questions.whyHard: The others hide digraphs (3 sounds each); only the blend-heavy 'splash' reaches 5: /s/-/p/-/l/-/a/-/sh/.

questions.prompt: Say the word **thank** slowly. How many separate sounds do you hear?
questions.hint: Break it up: the 'th' is one sound, but listen carefully to the ending 'nk' — it hides two sounds.
questions.whyHard: 'th' is 2 letters/1 sound while 'nk' is 2 letters/2 sounds, pulling the count in opposite directions.

questions.prompt: The word **cat** has 3 sounds. Which word below also has exactly **3** sounds?
questions.hint: Tap once per sound for each word. Remember 'ck' is a letter team that makes only one sound.
questions.whyHard: 'duck' looks like 4 letters but 'ck' collapses to one sound (3 total), while the blend words each have 4.

questions.prompt:
Read these four sentences. In which one should your voice take a short PAUSE in the middle?
1. The dog ran fast.
2. Yes, I can help you.
3. We went to the park.
4. She likes to read books.
questions.hint: Look for the little mark that tells your voice to stop for a beat.
questions.whyHard: All four are short simple sentences; only the comma signals the mid-sentence pause and the child must spot it.

questions.prompt:
Three of these sentences should be read in a happy, excited voice. Which ONE should be read in a sad voice?
1. Hooray, we won the game!
2. I lost my little dog.
3. Look at the big rainbow!
4. It is my birthday today!
questions.hint: Don't just look at the marks — think about how each sentence would make you feel.
questions.whyHard: Punctuation alone misleads; the child must read for meaning, since the sad line has no exclamation and the others do.

questions.prompt:
In which sentence is a character TALKING out loud, so you would read it in that character's voice?
1. Sam ate his lunch.
2. "I am so hungry," said Sam.
3. The cat was hungry too.
4. It was time to eat.
questions.hint: Look for the marks that go around the exact words someone says.
questions.whyHard: Every choice is about food and hunger; the child must notice the quotation marks, not the topic, to find the spoken line.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence out loud: I packed apples, grapes, and nuts. Where should your voice take short pauses?
choices: A short pause after each comma: apples, grapes, nuts
Only one pause at the very end
In the middle of each word
questions.correct: A short pause after each comma: apples, grapes, nuts
questions.hint: Each comma in a list is a tiny stop sign for your voice.
questions.whyHard: The child must apply the comma-pause rule to a three-item list, not just a single comma, and reject the tempting 'end only' choice.

questions.prompt: You are reading a story out loud so a friend can understand it. What is the BEST way to read?
choices: As fast as you can without stopping
So slow that each word takes a long time
Smoothly, not too fast and not too slow
Skip any word that looks hard
questions.correct: Smoothly, not too fast and not too slow
questions.hint: Think about the speed that helps a listener follow along.
questions.whyHard: The 'read super fast' distractor matches a common belief that fast means good, so the child must know appropriate rate, not maximum speed.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "The frog will ___ from rock to rock."  Choices: hop, hot, hog, hut
questions.hint: Sound out each word, then ask which one is an ACTION a frog can do in the sentence. Let the meaning of the whole sentence decide.
questions.whyHard: All four are look-alike CVC words a first grader can decode, so only context (a frog doing an action) rules out three.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "Mom will ___ the bread in the hot oven."  Choices: bake, cake, lake, rake
questions.hint: Read the whole sentence first. Which magic-e word names something you DO to bread in an oven?
questions.whyHard: These are near-identical magic-e (a_e) words; decoding alone can't choose, and 'rake' is even a real verb, so the oven/bread context is the only tie-breaker.

questions.prompt: Sam read this sentence out loud: "Our class went on a ___ to the farm."  He first said the word was 'trap,' but that did not make sense. Which word is really in the blank?  Choices: trip, trap, drip, trop
questions.hint: When a word doesn't make sense, look again at the letters AND ask what fits the sentence. What do you go ON to a farm?
questions.whyHard: It models self-correcting a real miscue ('trap' for 'trip'); the choices are one-letter-apart look-alikes, so the child must use meaning, not just sounds.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "The snake will ___ through the tall grass."  Choices: slide, slime, smile, slice
questions.hint: Blend the tricky sl-/sm- beginnings carefully, then keep only the word that tells how a snake MOVES.
questions.whyHard: The blends sl-/sm- and magic-e endings make these easy to confuse by sight; only the snake-moving context separates 'slide' from the other decodable words.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "The baby will ___ loudly when she is hungry."  Choices: cry, dry, try, fry
questions.hint: All four rhyme, so sounding out won't be enough. Ask which one a hungry baby would do loudly.
questions.whyHard: Four rhyming -ry words are all fully decodable, so the reader must lean entirely on the sentence's meaning to confirm the right word.

questions.prompt: One of these words has the /f/ sound even though there is NO letter 'f' in it. Which word is it?  graph  •  grab  •  grip  •  glad
questions.hint: Two letters can team up to make the /f/ sound. Say each word slowly and listen for the /f/ at the end.
questions.whyHard: The digraph 'ph' spells /f/, so the sound doesn't match the letters you see.

questions.prompt: Which word ends with the SAME /ch/ sound you hear at the start of 'chip'?  catch  •  cash  •  cast  •  cap
questions.hint: The /ch/ sound at the END of a short word is often spelled with three letters, not two. Say each word and listen for /ch/.
questions.whyHard: /ch/ here is spelled 'tch', a tricky spelling of the same sound taught as 'ch'.

questions.prompt: The word 'this' starts with a soft, buzzing /th/ (your voice turns on). Which word starts with that SAME buzzing /th/ — not the quiet /th/ in 'thin'?  them  •  thin  •  thick  •  thumb
questions.hint: Put your hand on your throat and say each word. The buzzing one makes your throat hum on the /th/.
questions.whyHard: 'th' spells two different sounds; only 'them' uses the voiced /th/ that matches 'this'.

questions.prompt: Three of these words begin with a DIGRAPH (two letters that make ONE sound). Which word does NOT?  shed  •  chin  •  whip  •  spin
questions.hint: A digraph makes one sound; a blend lets you still hear each letter. Stretch out the first two letters of each word.
questions.whyHard: Must tell a digraph (sh, ch, wh) apart from a blend (sp), where both letters are still heard.

questions.prompt: Which word has the /sh/ sound (like in 'ship')?  brush  •  catch  •  teach  •  much
questions.hint: Listen closely to the end of each word. /sh/ is a soft, hissing sound; /ch/ has a little punch to it.
questions.whyHard: The odd-one-out /sh/ must be picked from three close /ch/ words, forcing fine sh-vs-ch discrimination.

questions.prompt: Which word rhymes with 'branch'?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and listen only to the ending part after the first sound. Which ending sounds exactly like '-anch'?
questions.whyHard: 'ranch' shares the tricky -anch rime, while 'bench'/'bunch' are near-miss rimes and 'brush' shares the 'br-' start as a distractor.

questions.prompt: Three of these words begin with the SAME two-letter blend. Which word does NOT belong?
questions.hint: Stretch out the first two sounds of each word. Three start with /fl/ — find the one that starts a different way.
questions.whyHard: 'frog' begins with /fr/ while the others begin with /fl/, a one-sound difference that's easy to miss.

questions.prompt: Which word has the /ch/ sound you hear at the start of 'chin'?
questions.hint: Each word starts with two letters that make one sound. Only one makes the /ch/ sound like 'chin'.
questions.whyHard: All four options are digraph words (sh, ch, th, wh), so the child must discriminate /ch/ from three other look-alike two-letter blends.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME middle vowel sound as 'plum'?
questions.hint: Say each word and hold the middle sound. Which one has the short /u/ sound like the 'u' in 'plum'?
questions.whyHard: Every choice is a blend word with a different short vowel, forcing fine vowel discrimination rather than reading by shape.

questions.prompt: Which word has only ONE syllable?
questions.hint: Clap once for each vowel sound you HEAR, not for how long the word looks. A long word can still be one clap.
questions.whyHard: 'splash' looks long (six letters, three-consonant blend) but is one beat, while the shorter-looking choices are all two syllables.

questions.prompt: Read these four words: **gave, wave, have, cave**. Three of them use the Magic E to make the long A sound. Which word is the tricky one that has a SHORT vowel sound instead?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud. Listen for the one where the A does NOT say its name.
questions.whyHard: 'have' looks like a magic-e word but breaks the rule with a short vowel.

questions.prompt: Which word has the long A sound spelled with a vowel team? **rain, ran, ramp, rat**
questions.hint: Look for two vowels sitting together that team up to say the long A.
questions.whyHard: Only one word uses a vowel team; the close look-alikes all have a short a.

questions.prompt: Add a Magic E to the end of **plan**. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Keep all the letters of 'plan' and just add e at the end. Don't pick by sound alone.
questions.whyHard: 'plain' sounds the same but is a different spelling; the magic-e answer is 'plane'.

questions.prompt: The word **train** has the long A sound spelled with 'ai'. Which word has that SAME long A sound, but spelled a different way? **play, plan, pat, tap**
questions.hint: Find the word that also says long A, then check which letters make that sound.
questions.whyHard: Requires knowing long A can be spelled 'ai' OR 'ay' and matching by sound not letters.

questions.prompt: The word **boat** has the long O sound made by a vowel team. Which word has the SAME long O sound, but made by a Magic E instead? **rope, rock, rob, rot**
questions.hint: Say each word. Find the long O, then look for the silent e at the end.
questions.whyHard: Child must match one long-O spelling (oa) to a different long-O spelling (magic-e) among short-o traps.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **shine**?
questions.hint: Say the word slowly and count the vowel SOUNDS you hear, not the vowel letters you see. The 'e' at the end is silent.
questions.whyHard: Two vowel letters (i and e) tempt a count of 2, but silent-e makes just one vowel sound, so it is 1 syllable.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **boat**?
questions.hint: When two vowels sit together in a team, they usually make just one sound. Clap once for each vowel sound you hear.
questions.whyHard: The vowel team 'oa' has two vowel letters but makes a single vowel sound, so despite looking long it is only 1 syllable.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **apple**?
questions.hint: Break it into chunks and say each part out loud: ap - ple. Every chunk needs its own vowel sound.
questions.whyHard: The second syllable '-ple' hides its vowel sound with no clear vowel letter, so children may count it as one syllable.

questions.prompt: Which word has **TWO** syllables? **cake, robin, rain, kite**
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and count the vowel sounds. Watch out for silent-e words and vowel teams that only make one sound.
questions.whyHard: Three choices (cake, kite, rain) look like they hold two vowels but each is only one syllable; only 'robin' truly has two vowel sounds.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **cupcake**?
questions.hint: Clap the word in parts: cup - cake. Remember the silent 'e' does not add a beat.
questions.whyHard: It has three vowel letters (u, a, e), but the silent e means only two vowel sounds, so the tempting answer 'three' is wrong.

questions.prompt: How do you break the word **insect** into two syllables?
questions.hint: Find the two consonants side-by-side in the middle and split right between them.
questions.whyHard: A longer VCCV word where kids are tempted to split after the first single vowel or leave a lone letter.

questions.prompt: How do you break the word **muffin** into two syllables?
questions.hint: When two of the same letter sit in the middle, split right between the pair.
questions.whyHard: The double 'ff' tempts kids to keep both f's together or split in the wrong spot.

questions.prompt: Which two-syllable word splits between two DIFFERENT middle letters, like **nap-kin**?
questions.hint: Look at the two consonants in the middle of each word — are they the same letter twice, or two different letters?
questions.whyHard: Requires abstracting the pattern across words, not decoding one; every distractor hides a double-letter split.

questions.prompt: How do you break the word **walnut** into two syllables?
questions.hint: Say it slowly, clap the two parts, and split between the middle consonants.
questions.whyHard: A less-common two-syllable word with an l+n middle that kids rarely see split.

questions.prompt: Which of these words has TWO syllables? **bench**, **blast**, **scratch**, **kitten**
questions.hint: Clap each word and count the vowel sounds you actually hear — one clap per syllable.
questions.whyHard: The one-syllable blend words look long and letter-heavy, tempting an overcount, while the true two-syllable word looks short.

questions.prompt: What is the root word (base word) in **running**?
questions.hint: Take off the -ing ending, then remember one of the doubled letters is only there because of the ending.
questions.whyHard: The child must undo the consonant-doubling rule, not just strip -ing, so 'runn' is a tempting trap.

questions.prompt: In which word does the **-ed** ending sound like **/t/**, the way it does in **jumped**?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen only to the very last sound: is it a /t/, a /d/, or an extra 'id' beat?
questions.whyHard: Same -ed spelling makes three different sounds (/t/, /d/, /id/), so the child must judge by ear, not by letters.

questions.prompt: How do you correctly add the **-ed** ending to the word **cry** to make **cried**?
questions.hint: When a word ends in y after a consonant, the y changes before you add the ending.
questions.whyHard: Requires the y-to-i change; the phonetic spelling 'cryed' looks right if you don't know the rule.

questions.prompt: All of these words start with the /s/ sound. Three of them sound out exactly the way they are spelled. One is a TRICKY word that does NOT sound out that way. Which one is the tricky word?  sand  ·  silk  ·  said  ·  sock
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and blend the letters. Three will match their letters. The one that comes out sounding different is the tricky word.
questions.whyHard: All four share the same /s/ start, so the child must actually decode each and catch that 'said' breaks the rule (ai = short e).

questions.prompt: The tricky word that means a person you like and play with is said as 'frend.' Which spelling below is the correct way to write this tricky word?  frend  ·  friend  ·  freind  ·  frinnd
questions.hint: This tricky word has an 'i' hiding before the 'e' that you don't hear when you say it. Sounding it out won't help — you have to remember the order.
questions.whyHard: The 'ie' is silent-ordered and the sound-it-out spelling ('frend') is the trap, so the child can't rely on phonics.

questions.prompt: Three of these words rhyme with 'nose.' One is a TRICKY word that sounds different even though it looks the same. Which one is the tricky word?  goes  ·  toes  ·  does  ·  hose
questions.hint: Read each one out loud and listen for the odd one. Three will rhyme with each other. The one that sounds like 'duz' is the tricky word.
questions.whyHard: The spellings all look like the long-o rime, so the child must recognize by sound that 'does' is the irregular odd-one-out.

questions.prompt: The tricky word in 'Once upon a time' sounds like 'wunce.' Which spelling below is the correct way to write this tricky word?  wonce  ·  wunce  ·  once  ·  onse
questions.hint: You hear a /w/ at the start, but this tricky word does not begin with a 'w'. Remember how it looks, not how it sounds.
questions.whyHard: The word starts with a heard /w/ sound but is spelled with 'o', so the sound-it-out choices (wonce/wunce) are the tempting traps.

questions.prompt: In three of these words, the 'e' at the end makes the 'i' say its name, the long-i sound like in 'time.' One is a TRICKY word where the 'i' stays short. Which one is the tricky word?  hive  ·  give  ·  dive  ·  five
questions.hint: Try the magic-e rule on each: does the 'i' say its name? Three will. The one where the 'i' stays a short sound is the tricky word.
questions.whyHard: It's a silent-e exception — every word has the same _ive pattern, so only careful decoding reveals 'give' keeps a short i.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"Dogs need clean water every day. A dog that is thirsty can get sick. Please fill your dog's water bowl each morning."
Why did the author write this?
choices: To tell a funny story about a dog
To explain how dogs drink water
To tell readers what they should do to care for a dog
To describe what a dog looks like
questions.correct: To tell readers what they should do to care for a dog
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that asks the reader to DO something, not just a fact about dogs.
questions.whyHard: The text looks like plain facts, but the word 'Please' and 'each morning' signal a call to action, not just information.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"The moon does not make its own light. It shines because the sun's light bounces off it. That is why the moon looks bright at night."
What is the author's main purpose?
choices: To tell a story about the night sky
To make readers laugh about the moon
To describe how the sun feels warm
To explain why the moon looks bright
questions.correct: To explain why the moon looks bright
questions.hint: Ask what the WHOLE text is trying to help you understand, not just one word in it.
questions.whyHard: Two choices sound informational; the child must see the text explains a cause ('why') rather than describes or tells a story.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"First, wash your hands. Next, dry them well. Last, you can eat your snack."
Why did the author write this?
choices: To tell why hands get dirty
To show the steps to do something in order
To teach the names of foods
To explain why snacks taste good
questions.correct: To show the steps to do something in order
questions.hint: Notice the order words: First, Next, Last. What kind of writing uses those?
questions.whyHard: The word 'snack' can pull kids toward a food-themed choice, but the signal words show the purpose is to give ordered steps.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"Reading is the best thing to do at night. It helps your brain grow. You should read one book before bed."
What does the author want YOU to do?
questions.hint: One sentence tells a fact; another tells you what to do. Find the 'you should' part.
questions.whyHard: A true fact ('helps your brain grow') is a tempting choice, but the author's real want is the action the reader should take.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"Ants are very strong bugs. One ant can lift a leaf much bigger than its body. Ants work together to carry food back home."
Which sentence tells the BIG idea of the whole text?
choices: One ant can lift a leaf much bigger than its body
Ants work together to carry food back home
questions.hint: The big idea is the one all the other sentences give proof for.
questions.whyHard: The vivid detail about lifting a leaf feels important, but it is evidence FOR the big idea, not the big idea itself; one choice isn't even in the text.

questions.prompt: Which word has a long i sound? **stick, strike, string, script**
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. A silent e at the end often makes the vowel say its own name.
questions.whyHard: All four share tricky s-blends and the letter i, so the child must hear the magic-e long i, not just spot the vowel.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as **bread**? **bead, meant, dream, bean**
questions.hint: The letters 'ea' can say two sounds. Listen for the short e sound you hear in bread.
questions.whyHard: The 'ea' team spells two sounds; only 'meant' matches bread's short e while the rest are long e.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as **first**? **farm, nurse, form, fair**
questions.hint: Different letters can make the same r-controlled sound. Listen to the middle of 'first' and match it.
questions.whyHard: ir, ur, ar, or, and air are all r-controlled, so the child must hear that ir and ur share the /er/ sound.

questions.prompt: Which word starts with an open syllable that has a long vowel, like **tiger** (ti-ger)? **rabbit, robot, napkin, basket**
questions.hint: Clap the word into two parts. If the first part ends in a vowel, that vowel is usually long.
questions.whyHard: The child must split each word into syllables and notice only 'ro-bot' ends its first syllable on a vowel (open, long o).

questions.prompt: What is the base (root) word in **unhappily**?
questions.hint: Peel off the beginning part and the ending part. What word is left in the middle?
questions.whyHard: The child must strip both the prefix un- and suffix -ly and undo the y-to-i spelling change to reach 'happy', with 'happily' as a close trap.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **e** sound? Bread, Sweep, Deck, Kept
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does the vowel say its own name (like the /ee/ in 'see'), or is it a quick short sound? Watch out — the letters 'ea' don't always make a long sound.
questions.whyHard: All four words contain the letter 'e', and 'bread' is a trap because 'ea' usually spells long e but here it says short e.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **i** sound? Child, Print, Find, Might
questions.hint: Try each word: does the 'i' say its name (/eye/) or the quick /ih/ sound? Some words hide a long i even without a silent e.
questions.whyHard: Child, find, and might all say long i through irregular patterns (i before ld/nd, and igh), so only 'print' keeps the plain short i.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **a** sound? Draft, Sprain, Blast, Grand
questions.hint: Ignore the beginning blend and listen only to the middle vowel. Does 'a' say its name /ay/, or the short /a/ as in 'cat'?
questions.whyHard: The heavy beginning blends make every word look similar, but only the 'ai' team in 'sprain' spells the long a sound.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **u** sound? Trust, Prune, Brush, Crunch
questions.hint: Listen to the middle vowel after the blend. Does 'u' say its name (/oo/ as in 'tune') or the short /uh/ sound? A silent e at the end can change it.
questions.whyHard: All four begin with tough consonant blends and share the letter 'u', but only the silent-e in 'prune' makes the u long.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **o** sound? Coach, Frost, Float, Groan
questions.hint: Say each word and listen to the 'o'. Does it say its name /oh/, or the quick short /o/ as in 'dog'? The 'oa' team is a clue for one sound.
questions.whyHard: Three words use the 'oa' team for a long o, so the reader must spot that 'frost' is the only one with a plain short o.

questions.prompt: The vowel team **ea** usually says long e, like in **beach**. But it can also say short e, like in **head**. Which word below has **ea** saying the SHORT e sound?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud. Listen for the one where 'ea' sounds like the 'e' in 'bed,' not the 'e' in 'see.'
questions.whyHard: Tests the ea-team exception (short e) against three regular long-e words that use the same spelling.

questions.prompt: The vowel team **oo** makes two sounds. In **moon** it is long, but in **book** it is short. Which word has the SAME **oo** sound as **book**?
questions.hint: Say 'book,' then say each choice. Find the one whose 'oo' sounds just like it, not like 'zoo.'
questions.whyHard: Requires discriminating the two oo sounds when the spelling looks identical in every choice.

questions.prompt: The vowel team **ie** can say long i, like in **pie**, or long e, like in **chief**. Which word has **ie** saying the long e sound?
questions.hint: Read each word. Which one rhymes with a long-e word like 'shield,' not with 'my'?
questions.whyHard: The ie team has two sounds spelled the same way, so the child must decode each word to hear which is long e.

questions.prompt: The word **rain** makes its long a sound with the vowel team **ai**. Which word makes the long a sound with a DIFFERENT vowel team?
questions.hint: Three of these spell long a the same way as 'rain.' Look for the one that uses a-y instead of a-i.
questions.whyHard: Same long-a sound across all choices; only fine attention to the letters ai vs ay reveals the odd spelling.

questions.prompt: A vowel team is two vowels working together to make one sound, like **oa** or **ee**. Which word below has NO vowel team?
questions.hint: Look at the vowels in each word. Which word has only one single vowel with no partner next to it?
questions.whyHard: The odd-one-out is disguised among three real vowel-team words, forcing the child to inspect each spelling.

questions.prompt:
Three of these two-syllable words have a long vowel sound. Which word does NOT?

music   human   tulip   muffin
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and split it in two. If the first syllable ends in a vowel (mu-sic), that vowel is usually long. Which one has a closed first syllable with a short sound?
questions.whyHard: music and muffin both start with mu, but only one is an open syllable with a long u.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **o** sound?

robot   rocket   pocket   locket
questions.hint: Break each word after the first vowel or after the first consonant. If the first syllable ends in the vowel, the o stays long. Try ro-bot versus rock-et.
questions.whyHard: All four look alike, but only robot splits into an open syllable (ro-) that keeps the o long.

questions.prompt: Which shows the correct way to break **tiger** into two syllables so the **i** stays long?
questions.hint: When one consonant sits between two vowels, try splitting BEFORE the consonant first. That leaves the first vowel at the end of its syllable, so it says its long name.
questions.whyHard: The VCV pattern could split two ways, but only ti-ger leaves an open syllable that makes the i long.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **a** sound?

mistake   basket   napkin   rabbit
questions.hint: Read the second syllable of each word. Look for a silent e on the end that can jump back and make the a say its name.
questions.whyHard: The long a is hidden in the second syllable (mis-take) by a silent e, while the distractors are all short-a closed syllables.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **e** sound?

zebra   lemon   wagon   rabbit
questions.hint: Split each word into two parts. If the first syllable ends in the vowel (ze-bra), that vowel is long. Compare ze-bra with lem-on.
questions.whyHard: ze- and le- look similar, but only zebra's open first syllable makes the e long; lemon's is a short closed syllable.

questions.prompt: Read these four words: uncle, under, until, unpack. In which word is un- a PREFIX that means "not" or "reverse"?
questions.hint: Cover the un- and see if a real base word is left. "pack" is a word, but "cle," "der," and "til" are not.
questions.whyHard: Three distractors start with the letters u-n but are not the prefix un-, forcing true prefix analysis instead of letter-matching.

questions.prompt: The word grinned was made by adding -ed to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: When -ed was added, the last consonant was doubled. Take off -ed and one extra n to find the short base word.
questions.whyHard: Requires undoing consonant doubling, and "grind" is a real, tempting look-alike that never took a doubled -ed.

questions.prompt: The word riding was made by adding -ing to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: An e was dropped before adding -ing. Add the e back to the base, not just the letters you see.
questions.whyHard: "rid" is a real word that also makes an -ing form (ridding), so the child must know the dropped-e rule to reject it.

questions.prompt: The word cried was made by adding -ed to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: A y was changed to i before -ed was added. Change the i back to y to find the base word.
questions.whyHard: Tests the y-to-i spelling change; the correct base looks least like the finished word, so surface letter-matching fails.

questions.prompt: Read these four words: red, reptile, ready, rewrite. In which word is re- a PREFIX that means "again"?
questions.hint: Cover the re- and check for a real base word that could happen "again." "write" works; "d," "ptile," and "ady" do not.
questions.whyHard: All four begin with r-e, but only one splits into re- + a base word, so it targets prefix meaning, not spelling.

questions.prompt: In three of these words the letters **ea** make the long-e sound you hear in *eat*. Which word is the tricky one, where **ea** makes a SHORT-e sound instead?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen to the middle sound. Three match 'eat' — one sounds like 'bed'.
questions.whyHard: All four share the same 'ea' spelling, so the child must judge by ear, not by letters.

questions.prompt: The word **said** does not sound the way its letters look — it sounds like /sed/. Which word below **rhymes with said**?
questions.hint: Ignore how 'said' is spelled and listen to how it sounds when you talk. Which word ends with that same sound?
questions.whyHard: The 'ai' in 'said' breaks the rule, and the long-a distractors all match its spelling but not its sound.

questions.prompt: In the word **knot**, the first letter is silent — you only hear /not/. Which other word begins with a SILENT letter?
questions.hint: Say each word. Three start with the /n/ sound you can hear right away — one hides a silent letter before the 'n'.
questions.whyHard: 'knee' and 'nest' both sound like they start with /n/, so the child must know the silent-k spelling, not just the sound.

questions.prompt: Words that end in silent **e** usually make the vowel say its long name, like *bone* and *home*. Which word BREAKS that rule — it ends in **e** but the vowel is not long?
questions.hint: Say each word. Three make the 'o' say its name (long o). One sounds like 'kum' — that's the tricky one.
questions.whyHard: 'come' looks exactly like a magic-e word but disobeys the rule, hiding among three that follow it.

questions.prompt: The word **would** is tricky: the **l** is silent and it sounds like /wood/. Which word below **rhymes with would**?
questions.hint: Say 'would' out loud, then find the word that ends with the same sound — watch out for words that just look similar.
questions.whyHard: The child must hear the /oo/ in the '-ould' pattern and reject look-alikes like 'gold' that don't rhyme.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "for the reason that," as in "I stayed inside ___ it was raining." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This word breaks the rules. Picture the whole word the way you have seen it in books, and remember it ends with the little word 'cause.'
questions.whyHard: 'Because' cannot be sounded out letter by letter, and every wrong choice matches how it actually sounds.

questions.prompt: Three of these words follow the regular sounding-out rules. One is an irregular word you must know by heart because the letters do not make their usual sounds. Which one is the irregular word?
questions.hint: Try to sound out each word. The one where the 'ai' does NOT say its normal sound is the tricky one.
questions.whyHard: 'Said' looks like it should rhyme with 'paid,' so the child must catch that its 'ai' says a short-e sound instead.

questions.prompt: In one of these words the letters 'gh' make the /f/ sound (like in 'ph'). Which word is it?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen for an /f/ sound at the end. In the others the 'gh' is silent.
questions.whyHard: 'gh' is silent in most words, so the child must know the rare case where it flips to /f/.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "as much as you need," as in "I have ___ money." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This is an irregular word. It ends with 'gh' even though you hear an /f/ sound. Picture how it looks on the page.
questions.whyHard: The /f/ ending is spelled 'gh,' so the spellings that match the sound are all wrong.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "was able to," as in "I ___ hear the bird." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This word has a letter you do not hear. Remember it belongs to the 'could, would, should' family that all share the same tricky ending.
questions.whyHard: 'Could' hides a silent 'l' and an 'ou' that does not make its normal sound, so the phonetic-looking choices are traps.

questions.prompt:
Which of these words has TWO syllables? Read each one carefully:

through    yellow    bright    scratched
questions.hint: Clap the word out loud. Count the beats where your chin drops, not the letters you see.
questions.whyHard: 'scratched' and 'through' look long but are only one beat, so letter-count fools the eye.

questions.prompt:
Which word RHYMES with the word 'said'? (Say 'said' out loud first: it sounds like /sed/.)

seed    red    sad    side
questions.hint: Listen to the ending sound, not the spelling. Say 'said' slowly and match the last part.
questions.whyHard: 'said' is spelled with 'ai' but says short-e, so the spelling points to the wrong rhyme.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same long-i sound and silent-e pattern as 'stripe'?

slide    sniff    spill    sprint
questions.hint: Find the silent e on the end that makes the i say its name. Cover the blend and check the vowel.
questions.whyHard: The consonant blends hide the vowel, and three short-i words sit right next to the magic-e one.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same vowel sound as 'boil'? (Say 'boil': it has the /oy/ sound in the middle.)

toy    book    boat    bowl
questions.hint: The /oy/ sound can be spelled 'oi' or 'oy'. Listen for the sound, not the letters.
questions.whyHard: Same sound is spelled two ways (oi/oy), and the other 'oo/oa/ow' words look similar.

questions.prompt:
Which word BEGINS with the same sound as 'gentle'? (Say 'gentle': it starts with a /j/ sound.)

giant    goat    great    gum
questions.hint: The letter g can say /g/ or /j/. Say each word and listen to the very first sound.
questions.whyHard: Every choice starts with the letter g, but only one makes the soft /j/ sound.

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with the word great?

great
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen to the ending sound. Don't trust the spelling — 'ea' can trick you.
questions.whyHard: 'Great' uses an irregular 'ea' that says long-a, so it rhymes with 'plate' — not the three look-alike 'ea' words that all say long-e.

questions.prompt:
In which word does the letter c make the /s/ sound (like in 'ice')?

cent   cave   cup   cot
questions.hint: Look at the letter right after the c. C is soft (/s/) before e, i, or y, and hard (/k/) before a, o, or u.
questions.whyHard: All four words start with the same letter, but only the vowel that follows decides whether c is soft /s/ or hard /k/.

questions.prompt:
How many syllables are in the word important?

important
questions.hint: Clap once for each vowel sound you hear: im - por - tant. Count the beats, not the letters.
questions.whyHard: It's a longer word with an r-controlled middle chunk ('por'), which kids often blur into one beat and undercount.

questions.prompt:
Which word has a silent letter (a letter you do NOT hear when you say it)?

knee   nest   note   need
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does every letter make a sound? Watch the very first letter of each word.
questions.whyHard: All four begin with the /n/ sound, so the child must notice the silent 'k' hiding at the front of 'knee'.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same vowel sound as the word boy?

boy
questions.hint: Listen for the /oy/ sound in the middle. Remember that 'oi' and 'oy' can spell the very same sound.
questions.whyHard: The matching sound is spelled 'oi' instead of 'oy', while the distractors ('ow', 'o_e', 'oo') are close-but-different vowel sounds.

questions.prompt: Someone asks you: "WHO ate the last apple?" You answer by reading aloud: "Sam ate the last apple." Which word should you stress (say the strongest) to answer the question?
questions.hint: Stress the word that gives the exact information the question is asking for.
questions.whyHard: The child must match spoken stress to the question's focus instead of reading every word evenly.

questions.prompt: Read this line the right way: "We won!" she whispered. How should you read the part "We won!"?
choices: Softly, in a whisper, but excited
questions.correct: Softly, in a whisper, but excited
questions.hint: The words right after the quote (the tag) tell you HOW the person is speaking, even when there is an exclamation mark.
questions.whyHard: The exclamation mark tempts a loud read, but the tag word 'whispered' overrides it.

questions.prompt: Where should you take a short pause when you read this sentence aloud? "When the bell rang, the class lined up."
questions.hint: A comma is a signal to take a quick breath or pause.
questions.whyHard: Requires reading in meaning chunks and pausing at the comma, not at a random or every word.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence aloud: "You ate the whole thing?" It has no question word like "what" or "who," but it ends with a question mark. How should your voice sound at the end?
questions.hint: A question mark tells your voice to rise at the end, even when the sentence looks like a statement.
questions.whyHard: The statement-like wording tempts a falling, flat read, but the '?' means the voice must rise.

questions.prompt: You are reading a story aloud. It says: "The house was silent. Slowly, quietly, she opened the creaky door." How should you read this part?
choices: Slowly and quietly, to build suspense
questions.correct: Slowly and quietly, to build suspense
questions.hint: Let the mood words — silent, slowly, quietly — guide how fast and how loud you read.
questions.whyHard: The reader must infer pace and tone from the mood of the words instead of defaulting to a normal, even read.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "Yesterday I read a whole book about frogs." How should you say the word **read** here?
choices: Like "red," because the sentence says it already happened (yesterday)
Like "reed," because you are saying it right now
questions.correct: Like "red," because the sentence says it already happened (yesterday)
questions.hint: The word 'read' can sound two ways. Look for a clue in the sentence that tells you WHEN it happened.
questions.whyHard: Same spelling has two pronunciations; only the context word 'yesterday' tells you which sound is right.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The farmer will lead the cow to the barn." Which is the right way to say **lead**?
choices: It rhymes with "feed," because he is guiding the cow
It rhymes with "bed," because it is the heavy gray metal
questions.correct: It rhymes with "feed," because he is guiding the cow
questions.hint: 'Lead' can name a metal OR mean to guide someone. Reread the sentence to see which meaning fits the cow.
questions.whyHard: A heteronym: the letters are identical, so the child must use meaning in context to choose the vowel sound.

questions.prompt: A reader read this sentence: "Mom put the groceries in the trunk of the car." She first said the word **trunk** as "truck." What is the best fix?
choices: Reread it as 'trunk'—groceries go in a car's trunk, and the word ends in -nk
Keep 'truck' because it looks almost the same
Skip the word and read on
questions.correct: Reread it as 'trunk'—groceries go in a car's trunk, and the word ends in -nk
questions.hint: 'Truck' and 'trunk' look alike. Check the LAST sounds of the word and whether it makes sense in the car.
questions.whyHard: Truck and trunk differ by one letter; the child must confirm using both the ending sound and the sentence meaning.

questions.prompt: A reader read this sentence: "The library is a quiet place to read." He said the word **quiet** as "quite." What should he do?
choices: Reread it—'quiet' has two beats (qui-et), means no noise, and fits a library
Keep 'quite' because the letters look the same
questions.correct: Reread it—'quiet' has two beats (qui-et), means no noise, and fits a library
questions.hint: 'Quiet' and 'quite' use the same letters in a different order. Clap the syllables and think about what a library is like.
questions.whyHard: Quiet/quite are easy to swap; correcting requires syllable count plus context, not just the letters.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The brave knight rode his horse to the castle." A reader said the word **knight** as "night." Which fix is best?
choices: Keep 'knight'—it starts with a silent k and means a man in armor, which fits riding a horse
Change it to 'night,' the dark time of day
questions.correct: Keep 'knight'—it starts with a silent k and means a man in armor, which fits riding a horse
questions.hint: 'Knight' and 'night' sound exactly the same. Use the spelling (silent k) AND the meaning of the sentence to decide.
questions.whyHard: Homophones knight/night sound identical; only the silent-k spelling plus the horse-and-castle context confirm the word.

questions.prompt:
In which word is **un-** a real prefix (a word part added to the front) that you can peel off to leave a smaller word?

uncle  •  until  •  untie  •  under
questions.hint: Try covering the first two letters. If a whole real word is left, un- is a true prefix; if only leftovers remain, it is just part of the word.
questions.whyHard: All four words begin with the letters u-n, but only 'untie' splits into the prefix un- plus the base word 'tie'.

questions.prompt: What is the base (root) word inside **happiness**?
questions.hint: Peel off the suffix -ness, then remember that a base word ending in y often changes the y to i before the suffix is added.
questions.whyHard: The y in 'happy' was changed to i before -ness, so the correct root is spelled differently ('happy') than it looks inside the word ('happi').

questions.prompt:
Which word ends with the same **/shun/** sound as **nation**?

mountain  •  question  •  vacation  •  kitchen
questions.hint: Say each ending out loud and listen for /shun/, not /tin/ or /chun/. The -tion spelling only says /shun/ in some words.
questions.whyHard: The endings -tain, -stion, -tion, and -tchen look and sound close, and 'question' is even spelled with -tion but says /chun/, so only 'vacation' truly ends in /shun/.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct when you add **-ing** to the word **hop**?
questions.hint: A one-syllable word with one short vowel and one final consonant (like hop) doubles that consonant before -ing. Watch out for the word 'hope'.
questions.whyHard: The short-vowel word 'hop' must double the p to keep the short /o/ sound, while the trap 'hoping' actually comes from the long-vowel word 'hope'.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **explain**?
questions.hint: Clap it out. Remember that a vowel team like 'ai' makes just one vowel sound, so it counts as only one syllable.
questions.whyHard: The two vowels a-i sit side by side and tempt kids to count an extra syllable, but 'ai' is a single vowel team, so ex-plain is only two beats.

questions.prompt: In which word does **un-** work as a real prefix meaning 'not'?  **unkind** · **uncle** · **under** · **uncover**
questions.hint: A real prefix peels off and leaves a whole base word that still makes sense. Take off un- and ask if what's left flips to the opposite.
questions.whyHard: Three choices contain the letters u-n, but only one is truly the prefix un- meaning 'not' (uncover means 'reverse,' uncle/under have no prefix).

questions.prompt: The suffix **-er** can mean 'a person who does something.' In which word does **-er** mean that?  **painter** · **colder** · **faster** · **longer**
questions.hint: -er has two jobs: comparing (bigger) or naming a doer (baker). Ask which word names a person.
questions.whyHard: All four words end in -er, but three are comparisons and only one names 'one who does' the action.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-less** means 'without.' Which word means 'without fear'?  **fearful** · **fearless** · **feared** · **fearing**
questions.hint: Match the meaning 'without' to the right ending: -less means without, -ful means full of.
questions.whyHard: Every choice is built from the same base 'fear,' so the child must attach 'without' to the exact suffix, not just spot the word.

questions.prompt: The prefix **pre-** means 'before.' What does the word **preheat** mean?  **to heat again** · **to heat before** · **to not heat** · **to heat fully**
questions.hint: Swap the prefix for its meaning, then read the base word right after it.
questions.whyHard: Each distractor matches a different affix meaning (re-, un-, -ful), so only knowing pre- specifically gives the answer.

questions.prompt: The prefix **mis-** means 'wrongly' or 'badly.' What does the word **misspell** mean?  **to spell again** · **to spell wrongly** · **to spell before** · **to spell fully**
questions.hint: Replace mis- with its meaning and keep the base word 'spell' after it.
questions.whyHard: The distractors each fit a different common prefix (re-, pre-, -ful), so only the exact meaning of the less-common mis- works.

questions.prompt: All four words end with the /shun/ sound. In which word is that ending spelled with the letters -sion?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and picture the ending. Three of them spell /shun/ with a t, but one uses an s.
questions.whyHard: Every choice ends in the same /shun/ sound, so the child must know the spelling, not just the sound.

questions.prompt: The Latin suffix -ous means 'full of' and sounds like /us/. Which word below actually ends with the suffix -ous?
questions.hint: Cover the last part of each word. Is what is left a real base word, or is /us/ just part of the whole word?
questions.whyHard: The distractors end in the same /us/ sound but are not the suffix, forcing true suffix recognition.

questions.prompt: The letters -tion usually say /shun/, as in 'nation.' In which word do the letters -tion say /chun/ instead?
questions.hint: Read each word out loud and listen for the one where your tongue makes a /ch/ catch right before the ending.
questions.whyHard: 'Question' is an exception where -tion says /chun/ after the s, breaking the normal rule.

questions.prompt: Read these four words. Which one ends with the Latin suffix -ible?
questions.hint: You cannot hear the difference between -able and -ible, so picture each word spelled out and check the vowel before -ble.
questions.whyHard: -able and -ible sound identical, so the sound gives no clue and the child must recall the spelling.

questions.prompt: The suffix -sion can say /shun/ or /zhun/ (the soft sound in 'treasure'). In which word does -sion make the /zhun/ sound?
questions.hint: Look at the letter right before -sion. A vowel there softens the ending to /zhun/.
questions.whyHard: /zhun/ only occurs when -sion follows a vowel; all three distractors follow a consonant and say /shun/.

questions.prompt:
All four of these words begin with the letters 'sw'. Which one has a SILENT letter you do not say out loud?

sword    swing    sweet    swan
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. In one of them the 'w' disappears and you jump straight to the next sound.
questions.whyHard: Every choice looks like it should sound the 'w', but only 'sword' hides a silent letter.

questions.prompt:
In the word 'listen', one of the letters is silent — you do not hear it when you say the word. Which letter is silent?

l-i-s-t-e-n
questions.hint: Say the word out loud and listen for a /t/ sound. If you can't hear it, that letter is the silent one.
questions.whyHard: The silent 't' sits in the middle of the word, so it's easy to assume you say it.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence: 'I bought one ___ of new shoes.'

Which word is spelled correctly to fill the blank?
questions.hint: All the choices sound almost the same. Think about which spelling means 'two things that go together.'
questions.whyHard: Three choices are homophones that sound identical, so the child must match spelling to meaning, not sound.

questions.prompt:
The letters 'gh' can be tricky — sometimes they make a sound and sometimes they are silent. In which word do the letters 'gh' make NO sound at all?

eight    ghost    laugh    tough
questions.hint: Say each word. In some, 'gh' sounds like /g/ or /f/. Find the one where 'gh' is completely silent.
questions.whyHard: 'gh' behaves three different ways across the choices, so the child must test each one, not guess.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence: 'I ___ like to come and play.'

Which word is spelled correctly and has a silent 'l'?
questions.hint: The right word means 'want to' and hides a silent 'l' in the middle. Watch out for a look-alike that means part of a tree.
questions.whyHard: 'wood' is a real word that sounds the same, tempting the child away from the irregular 'would'.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SOFT g sound (the /j/ sound, like in 'giraffe')?
questions.hint: The letter g is usually soft when it comes right before an e, i, or y. Look at what letter follows each g.
questions.whyHard: All four start with g, but only one has g followed by an i to make the soft /j/ sound, and the child must ignore the hard g in the rest of the word.

questions.prompt: Which word BEGINS with a silent letter, like the k in 'knee'?
questions.hint: Some words start with two consonants where the first one is not spoken. Say each word slowly and listen for a letter you write but don't hear.
questions.whyHard: All four are short decodable words with similar sounds, but only one hides a silent w at the start (wr), which is easy to miss.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word 'calculator'?
questions.hint: Clap or tap once for each vowel sound you hear: cal-cu-la-tor.
questions.whyHard: It is a long four-part word, and children often miscount by lumping two syllables together or splitting one, so it takes careful listening to each vowel sound.

questions.prompt: In which word does the letter 'a' make the schwa sound (the lazy /uh/ sound, like in 'about')?
questions.hint: The schwa is a quiet /uh/ sound in an unstressed part of a word. Say each word and notice where the 'a' sounds like a clear short /a/ versus a mumbled /uh/.
questions.whyHard: Three words have a clear short /a/, so the child must hear that only banana softens its a into the tricky schwa /uh/ sound.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as 'moon'?
questions.hint: Say 'moon' and stretch the middle sound. Some 'oo' words make that long sound and some make a shorter /oo/ sound. Listen for the exact match, even if it is spelled differently.
questions.whyHard: Three of the choices use oo but make the SHORT /oo/ sound, while the correct answer spells the long /oo/ of 'moon' a different way (ou), forcing sound-not-spelling matching.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SOFT g sound — the /j/ sound you hear in "gem"?
questions.hint: A soft g usually comes before e, i, or y. Say each word and listen for /j/ like in "gem" versus the hard /g/ in "go."
questions.whyHard: All four words begin with the letter g, so the child must judge the SOUND the g makes, not just the letter.

questions.prompt: The word "napkin" breaks into two syllables between the two middle consonants: nap-kin. Which word below breaks apart the SAME way, between two consonants?
questions.hint: Find the two consonants sitting between the vowels and split right between them. If there is only one consonant, the word usually splits after the first vowel instead.
questions.whyHard: The three wrong words each have a single middle consonant and split after the vowel (ro-bot), so only one truly matches the two-consonant nap-kin pattern.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as "field" (long e)?
questions.hint: The letters 'ie' don't always make the same sound. Say each word aloud and match its middle sound to the long e in "field."
questions.whyHard: "friend" is spelled with the very same 'ie' as "field" but says short e, a classic spelling trap.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SILENT letter — a letter you spell but do not say?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly, sound by sound. If a written letter never gets a sound, that word has a silent letter.
questions.whyHard: "drum" rhymes with "thumb" but has no silent b, so the child must check each letter's sound instead of trusting the rhyme.

questions.prompt: In the word "learn," the letters 'ear' make the /er/ sound, like in "her." Which word below has that SAME /er/ sound?
questions.hint: The letters 'ear' can make three different sounds. Say each word and listen for the /er/ in "her."
questions.whyHard: All four words are spelled with 'ear,' but it says /er/, /ar/, and /eer/ in different words, so spelling alone cannot pick the answer.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence. It is written on two lines:
"Sam has a pet.
 We like him."
You just finished reading "pet" at the end of the first line. Which word do you read NEXT?
questions.hint: At the end of a line, your eyes sweep DOWN and all the way back to the far LEFT to start the next line.
questions.whyHard: The next word is not the one to the right nearby, it is all the way back on the left of the line below (the return sweep).

questions.prompt:
This sentence is written on two lines:
"A fox can run
 very fast today."
Which word do you read LAST?
questions.hint: Reading a page ends at the very bottom, on the far right side of the last line.
questions.whyHard: The last word is at the bottom-right, not the last word of the top line, so the child must track to the true end.

questions.prompt: You open your book to a brand-new page that is full of words. Which word do you read FIRST?
choices: The word at the top on the left
The word at the top on the right
The word at the bottom on the left
The biggest word on the page
questions.correct: The word at the top on the left
questions.hint: Words start at the top of the page, and each line starts on the left side.
questions.whyHard: Two rules combine at once (top before bottom AND left before right), and size is a tempting wrong clue.

questions.prompt: A page has three short lines of words. You just finished reading the TOP line. Which line do you read next, and where do you start it?
choices: The middle line, starting on the LEFT
The middle line, starting on the RIGHT
The bottom line, starting on the LEFT
The top line again, starting on the LEFT
questions.correct: The middle line, starting on the LEFT
questions.hint: Go straight down to the very next line, then jump back to its far left before you read.
questions.whyHard: It pairs line order (next line, not skipping to the bottom) with the return-sweep to the left in one choice.

questions.prompt: Here is a word: w - a - s. To read it the correct way (was), which letter do you say FIRST?
questions.hint: Always read the letters left to right, starting at the very first letter on the left.
questions.whyHard: Reading left to right inside the word stops it from being flipped into 'saw', a classic K reversal trap.

questions.prompt: The letters d, o, and g can spell the word 'dog.' Which row shows the letters in the RIGHT order to spell 'dog'?
questions.hint: Say the word slowly and match each sound to its letter, left to right. All rows have the same letters, so the ORDER is what matters.
questions.whyHard: Every choice uses the exact same three letters, so the child must track the letter sequence, not just spot the right letters.

questions.prompt: The words 'pan' and 'nap' are built from the very same three letters: p, a, n. Why are they two DIFFERENT words?
choices: The letters are in a different order
One word has different letters than the other
One word is longer than the other
They are really the same word
questions.correct: The letters are in a different order
questions.hint: Look closely at both words. Do they use different letters, or the same letters arranged differently?
questions.whyHard: Both words share identical letters, so the child must realize sequence alone changes the word — a subtle idea, not a letter count.

questions.prompt: Which pair of words is made from the SAME three letters, just put in a different order?
questions.hint: Check if BOTH words use the exact same letters. In some pairs one letter is swapped for a new one instead of reordered.
questions.whyHard: The distractors change one letter (which feels similar), while only the correct pair truly reuses the same letters in a new order.

questions.prompt: The word 'bat' is spelled b-a-t. Which ONE letter would you change to make it spell 'bit'?
questions.hint: Line the two words up letter by letter: b-a-t and b-i-t. Which spot is different?
questions.whyHard: The child must compare two words letter-by-letter and pinpoint the single position that differs, not just hear that they sound alike.

questions.prompt: You hear the word 'pin.' Which row of letters spells the word 'pin'?
questions.hint: Stretch the word: /p/ /i/ /n/. Check the first sound, middle sound, AND last sound against each row.
questions.whyHard: Each wrong choice differs by only one letter (bin, pen, pim), forcing careful sound-by-sound matching to the written letters.

questions.prompt:
In print, each word has a space around it. Count the words in this line:

the big cat sat on a mat
questions.hint: Touch each word one at a time. Every space starts a new word. Don't stop early!
questions.whyHard: A long 7-word line where careful one-by-one counting is easy to lose track of.

questions.prompt:
Spaces go BETWEEN the words. How many spaces are between the words in this line?

red hot sun
questions.hint: Count the words first, then the gaps. There is always one less gap than there are words.
questions.whyHard: Counting spaces, not words, so the child can't just count the words and give that number.

questions.prompt: Which line puts a space between every word so we can tell the words apart?
questions.whyHard: Distractors have real words but wrong space placement, testing true word boundaries.
questions.hint: Read each choice slowly. A space should come exactly where one whole word ends and the next begins.

questions.prompt:
A space is missing in this line. Where should the space go to make TWO real words?

sundog
questions.hint: Say the line out loud and listen for two little words you know. Break it where one word ends.
questions.whyHard: The child must find the hidden word boundary inside run-together letters, not just count.

questions.prompt: Only one of these lines has exactly 4 words. Which one is it?
questions.hint: Count the words in each line by tapping every space. Look for the line that lands on 4.
questions.whyHard: Requires comparing word counts across several lines instead of counting just one.

questions.prompt: Look carefully! Which one of these is the lowercase letter "d"?
questions.hint: The letter d has its round belly on the LEFT side and the tall stick on the right — trace the circle first, then find where the line stands.
questions.whyHard: All four choices are the same circle-plus-stick shape just flipped or turned, so the child must track orientation, not just the parts.

questions.prompt: This capital letter is "G". Which lowercase letter goes with it?
questions.hint: Say the letter names out loud. Which little letter is named "gee"? All of these dip below the line, so listen for the NAME, not just the tail.
questions.whyHard: Every distractor also has a tail that hangs below the line, so the usual "look for the tail" trick fails and the child must actually name the letter.

questions.prompt: Which one shows a CAPITAL letter matched with its correct lowercase partner?
questions.hint: Check each pair by saying both letters' names. Only ONE pair says the SAME name twice (like "Are, are").
questions.whyHard: Three pairs mix up look-alike letters (B/d, N/m, Q/p), so the child must reject tempting almost-matches and find the true same-name pair.

questions.prompt: This lowercase letter is "r". Which CAPITAL letter matches it?
questions.hint: The lowercase r and capital R don't look much alike, so say the letter name "arr" and pick the big letter with the same name.
questions.whyHard: Lowercase r and capital R have very different shapes, and the distractors are curvy capitals a child might guess by shape instead of name.

questions.prompt: This lowercase letter is "w". Which CAPITAL letter matches it?
questions.hint: Watch out — one choice is just "w" turned upside down! Say the name "double-u" and pick the matching big letter.
questions.whyHard: Capital M is a flipped W, a classic visual trap, so the child must match by name rather than by the similar zig-zag shape.

questions.prompt:
Three of these words rhyme. Which word does NOT rhyme with the others?
cat, hat, hot, bat
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and listen to the ending sound. Three of them end the same way — find the one that ends differently.
questions.whyHard: The odd word 'hot' shares its first sound and looks almost like the rhyming words, so kids must judge the ending, not the look.

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'chair'?
bear, chin, cheer, chip
questions.hint: Listen for the ending sound in 'chair.' Some words that start like 'chair' do NOT rhyme with it — pick the one whose ending sound matches.
questions.whyHard: The rhyme 'bear' is spelled differently, while three trap words all start with 'ch' like 'chair' but don't rhyme.

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'sun'?
sit, sad, bun, sock
questions.hint: Rhymes match the ENDING sound, not the beginning. Say each word and listen to how it ends.
questions.whyHard: Three distractors all begin with /s/ like 'sun,' tempting kids to match the start instead of the ending rime.

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'cake'?
cane, lake, cape, cat
questions.hint: Say 'cake' and stretch its ending. Look for the word that ends with that same '-ake' sound.
questions.whyHard: 'cane' and 'cape' share the same magic-e look but a different ending sound, and 'cat' shares the /k/ start — only 'lake' truly rhymes.

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'ten'?
tan, tin, hen, top
questions.hint: Focus on the middle and ending sounds. A tiny change in the vowel can break a rhyme, so listen closely.
questions.whyHard: 'tan' and 'tin' differ from 'ten' by only one vowel sound, forcing fine vowel discrimination to reject the near-rhymes.

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "kite"?
questions.hint: Say the word slowly and feel your chin drop. The 'e' at the end is silent — it does not make its own clap.
questions.whyHard: The silent-e ending tempts kids to add a second clap, but 'kite' is one beat.

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "turtle"?
questions.hint: Clap it out: tur...tle. The '-le' chunk hides a beat even though you can't hear a clear vowel.
questions.whyHard: The '-le' ending has no obvious vowel sound, so the second syllable is easy to miss.

questions.prompt: Which word has ONLY ONE syllable (one clap)?
questions.hint: Clap each word out loud. Three of them break into two beats — find the one that stays a single clap.
questions.whyHard: 'snake' has a silent-e so it looks long, yet it is the one-clap word hiding among three two-syllable words.

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "jumped"?
questions.hint: Say it the way you talk, not letter by letter. The '-ed' just adds a /t/ sound here — no new clap.
questions.whyHard: The '-ed' ending fools kids into a second clap, but 'jumped' is spoken as one beat.

questions.prompt: Which word has TWO syllables (two claps)?
questions.hint: Clap each word. Watch out — a silent 'e' can make a short word LOOK longer than it sounds.
questions.whyHard: 'grape' has an extra vowel letter that mimics a second syllable, so kids may pick it over the true two-clap 'pencil'.

questions.prompt: Blend the onset and rime together: /f/ ... 'ish'. What word do you hear?
questions.hint: Say the first sound, then hold the ending part 'ish', and slide them together slowly.
questions.whyHard: The rime 'ish' ends in the digraph /sh/, so the child must blend a rime with a two-letter sound, and 'wish' shares the same rime as a near-distractor.

questions.prompt: Blend these parts into one word: /st/ ... 'op'. What word do they make?
questions.hint: The first part has TWO sounds blended, /s/ then /t/. Say them, then add 'op'.
questions.whyHard: The onset is a consonant blend (/st/), so the child must blend two onset sounds before joining the rime; 'top' uses only part of the onset as a trap.

questions.prompt: Say the word 'clap' in two parts: the beginning sounds and the rest. Which shows the correct onset and rime?
questions.hint: The onset is ALL the sounds before the first vowel. Find the vowel in 'clap', then split right before it.
questions.whyHard: This asks the child to SEGMENT (not blend) a word with a blend onset, and split exactly at the vowel — 'lap' wrongly keeps an onset sound in the rime.

questions.prompt: Blend the onset and rime: /ch/ ... 'in'. What word do you hear?
questions.hint: The first sound is one sound made by two letters: /ch/. Say it, then blend on 'in'.
questions.whyHard: The onset is the digraph /ch/, and 'shin' and 'thin' are near-rhymes with different single-digraph onsets, forcing fine beginning-sound discrimination.

questions.prompt: Put the onset and rime together: /g/ ... 'ift'. What word do they make?
questions.hint: Hold the ending part 'ift' — it has three sounds. Add /g/ to the front and blend it all.
questions.whyHard: The rime 'ift' is a three-sound rime ending in a blend (/f/ /t/), and 'lift' shares that whole rime with a different onset as a close trap.

questions.prompt: Say the word "web" slowly: /w/ /e/ /b/. Which word has the SAME middle sound as "web"?
questions.hint: Stretch each word and listen only to the vowel sound in the middle, not the beginning or end.
questions.whyHard: All four choices start with /t/ and are CVC, so the child must ignore the matching beginning and discriminate the medial vowel /e/ alone.

questions.prompt: Which word ends with the /k/ sound at the very end?
questions.hint: Listen to the LAST sound only. The 'ck' spelling makes one /k/ sound.
questions.whyHard: 'duck' hides the /k/ under a two-letter 'ck' spelling, and 'dug'/'dust' are close lookalike CVC decoys that end in different sounds.

questions.prompt: Say "cot" and "cut." They share the first sound /k/ and the last sound /t/. Which word has the SAME middle sound as "cut"?
questions.hint: Focus on the vowel in the very middle of the word, and match it to the /u/ you hear inside "cut."
questions.whyHard: Requires isolating the short /u/ against three near-minimal-pair vowels (/a/, /o/, /e/) in matching CVC frames.

questions.prompt: Which word has the /f/ sound at the BEGINNING?
questions.hint: Say the first sound out loud. Ask: does it start with the /f/ sound, even if it isn't spelled with an f?
questions.whyHard: 'phone' spells /f/ with 'ph' while decoy 'van' begins with the confusable /v/, so the child must match sound, not letter.

questions.prompt: Which word has the /o/ sound (like in "hot") in the MIDDLE?
questions.hint: All four start with /j/. Ignore the first sound and listen to just the vowel in the middle.
questions.whyHard: Four /j/-initial CVC words force pure medial-vowel discrimination among /o/, /a/, /e/, /u/ with no beginning-sound clue.

questions.prompt: Say the word 'pin'. Now change the MIDDLE sound, /i/, to the /a/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Keep the first sound /p/ and the last sound /n/ the same. Only the middle vowel changes.
questions.whyHard: Swaps the medial vowel (not the easy first sound), and 'pen' is a near-miss with a different vowel.

questions.prompt: Say the word 'bat'. Now change the LAST sound, /t/, to the /g/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Keep 'ba-' at the start and only change the ending sound.
questions.whyHard: Requires isolating and swapping the final consonant while 'bad' offers a close wrong ending.

questions.prompt: Say the word 'lip'. Now ADD the /s/ sound to the very front. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Put the new /s/ sound before the rest of the word, then blend it all together.
questions.whyHard: Adding a sound to build an s-blend is harder than a simple swap, and clip/flip are other real blends.

questions.prompt: Say the word 'cot'. Now change the FIRST sound, /k/, to the /h/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Keep the '-ot' ending and only change the beginning sound.
questions.whyHard: 'hat' shares the /h/ start but changes the rime, tempting a listener who ignores the ending.

questions.prompt: Look at these pairs. In which pair is the new word made by changing ONLY the middle sound?
questions.hint: Check that the first sound and the last sound stay the same, and only the vowel in the middle changes.
questions.whyHard: All four pairs share 'hop'; only one differs by exactly the medial vowel, forcing careful sound-by-sound comparison.

questions.prompt: The word "gate" starts with the /g/ sound (like "go"). Which word below starts with the SAME /g/ sound as "gate"?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Listen for the hard /g/ sound at the very start, like in "go" — not a soft /j/ sound.
questions.whyHard: The letter G makes a hard /g/ AND a soft /j/ sound; three distractors start with G but say /j/.

questions.prompt: The word "kite" starts with the /k/ sound. One of these words ALSO starts with the /k/ sound but is spelled with a C. Which one?
questions.hint: Two letters can make /k/. Say each word — "city" starts with C but says /s/. Which one truly begins with /k/?
questions.whyHard: Requires knowing C can say /k/ or /s/, so the child must use the sound, not just the letter.

questions.prompt: Say these words out loud: "fish," "fun," "vase," "fan." Which word does NOT start with the /f/ sound?
questions.hint: Put your top teeth on your bottom lip. /f/ is a quiet, breathy sound; /v/ buzzes. Which word buzzes instead?
questions.whyHard: /f/ and /v/ are made the same way in the mouth (only voicing differs), so they are easy to confuse by ear.

questions.prompt: The word "win" starts with the /w/ sound. Which word below starts with the SAME /w/ sound as "win"?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Listen for the /w/ sound made by your lips, and don't confuse /w/ with /v/ or /y/.
questions.whyHard: "wheel" is spelled wh but says /w/, while distractors /v/, /y/, /r/ are sounds young readers often confuse with /w/.

questions.prompt: Say these words: "jam," "gem," "dog," "gum." Which word does NOT start with the /g/ sound (like in "go")?
questions.hint: Careful — the question asks which does NOT start with /g/. Some of these start with /j/ or /d/, and G can trick you by saying /j/.
questions.whyHard: G can say /j/, and words start with /j/ and /d/, so the child must sort overlapping consonant sounds by ear, not by letter.

questions.prompt: All of these words have a magic 'e' at the end. Three of them make the vowel say its LONG name. Which word does NOT have a long vowel sound? "cane", "cone", "have", "cube"
questions.hint: Try saying each word out loud. Does the vowel say its own name (long) or a quick sound (short)? Listen for the odd one out.
questions.whyHard: 'have' is a magic-e exception with a short vowel, breaking the pattern the other three follow.

questions.prompt: The word "go" has a long 'o' sound. Which of these words also has the LONG 'o' sound? "got", "no", "box", "fox"
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does the 'o' say its name like in 'go', or make the short sound like in 'hot'?
questions.whyHard: Only 'no' keeps the long 'o'; the other three are short-o CVC words that look similar.

questions.prompt: Two words below use a DIFFERENT spelling but make the very same long 'e' sound as in "me". Which word makes the LONG 'e' sound? "bed", "see", "pet", "ten"
questions.hint: The letters 'ee' can spell the long 'e' sound. Say each word and listen for the one that says the letter name 'e'.
questions.whyHard: Requires recognizing the 'ee' spelling of long e while rejecting three short-e distractors.
